jokes.text: Hey, Homer, how come you got money to burn? Or singe, anyway.
jokes.setup: Standard 'money to burn' idiom about having excess money
jokes.punchline: Barney's literal interpretation with 'singe' as a weaker alternative
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay that fits Barney's slightly dim character perfectly. Economy is excellent with the quick pivot from burn to singe.

jokes.text: Pumpkins? Yeah, that's right, Barney. This year, I invested in pumpkins. They've been going up the whole month of October. And I got a feeling they're gonna peak right around January.
jokes.setup: Homer's investment strategy sounds sophisticated
jokes.punchline: The complete ignorance of seasonal produce economics - pumpkins peak in January
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - confidently wrong about something obvious. The seasonal timing is absurdly backwards, and it perfectly captures his character.

jokes.text: And bang! That's when I'll cash in. To Homer! And to Sergeant Pepper... who's growing out of the middle of your back!
jokes.setup: Normal celebratory toast
jokes.punchline: Barney's random observation about something bizarre on Homer's back
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons visual absurdity. The casual way Barney mentions something impossible growing on Homer is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Barn, you gotta unwrap the plastic before you smoke these.
jokes.setup: Someone smoking cigars normally
jokes.punchline: Barney is literally smoking plastic-wrapped cigars
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Barney's obliviousness, with efficient visual comedy setup.

jokes.text: I told you a hundred times, you gotta sell your pumpkin futures before Halloween.
jokes.setup: Homer's disastrous pumpkin investment from earlier
jokes.punchline: Moe stating the obvious that everyone except Homer knows
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that emphasizes Homer's ignorance while giving Moe a great deadpan moment.

jokes.text: Let's not panic. I'll make the money back by selling a liver. I can get by with one.
jokes.setup: Normal response to financial crisis would be panic
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual suggestion of organ sales as a viable financial strategy
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that perfectly captures Homer's twisted priorities and lack of understanding about human anatomy. The casual delivery sells it.

jokes.text: My house is on fire! Insurance to the rescue!
jokes.setup: Homer needs money desperately
jokes.punchline: His solution is to commit insurance fraud by burning down his own house
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer criminal thinking - he announces his plan like it's brilliant while committing obvious crimes.

jokes.text: Marge, we had a deal. Your sisters don't come here after 6, and I stop eating your lipstick.
jokes.setup: Normal household agreement about visiting relatives
jokes.punchline: Homer's bizarre reciprocal obligation to stop eating makeup
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor that reveals Homer's weird habits while framing it as a reasonable trade-off. Highly quotable and memorably bizarre.

jokes.text: All right, that's the last straw. Time to take out the trash! But first, I'm going to have to ask you to leave.
jokes.setup: Homer threatens to take out the trash aggressively
jokes.punchline: The polite redirect that he means asking them to leave
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Homer building up to aggression then deflating into politeness. Structure works well.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. Homer doesn't mean to be rude. He's just a very complicated man. Wrong!
jokes.setup: Marge defending Homer as complex and misunderstood
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately contradicting her charitable interpretation
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and character dynamics. Homer's immediate self-sabotage of Marge's defense is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: When are you going to wake up and smell your husband, Marge? You got some kids out of him. But when the seeds have been planted, you throw away the envelope.
jokes.setup: Relationship advice about Homer's usefulness
jokes.punchline: The bizarre seed packet metaphor comparing Homer to disposable packaging
jokes.explanation: Creative extended metaphor that perfectly captures the twins' disdain for Homer while being absurdly specific.

jokes.text: Hello, Vegas? Give me a hundred bucks on red. All right. I'll send you a check.
jokes.setup: Homer gambling by phone
jokes.punchline: The impossibility of phone gambling with payment by mail
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's complete misunderstanding of how gambling works, with efficient setup and payoff.

jokes.text: Congratulations, Mr. Simpson. This invention of yours has made us all rich, especially you. It's simple, yet ingenious. And it fits right in the palm of your hand. Every person in America now owns one of these. In many cases, three or four.
jokes.setup: Homer's invention is described in glowing but vague terms
jokes.punchline: The complete vagueness prevents us from knowing what it actually is
jokes.explanation: Clever dream sequence that parodies business speak while maintaining perfect ambiguity about the actual product.

jokes.text: Dream sequence where Homer invents something but can't see it
jokes.setup: Homer dreams of inventing something successful
jokes.punchline: The frustrating dream logic where he can't see his own invention
jokes.explanation: Relatable dream frustration turned into visual comedy

jokes.text: Why would you need to see it? You're the genius who invented the product.
jokes.setup: Homer asks to see his own invention in the dream
jokes.punchline: Dream logic prevents him from knowing what he supposedly invented
jokes.explanation: Good dream logic joke about not being able to see details in dreams

jokes.text: And who could forget dear Rat Boy? Rat Boy? I resent that. Bart, I told you before. Stop gnawing on the drywall.
jokes.setup: Homer giving affectionate nicknames to his family
jokes.punchline: Bart's nickname is explained by his literally chewing the walls
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from cute family moment to disturbing behavior, perfectly capturing both characters' dynamics.

jokes.text: The sun is out, birds are singing... bees are trying to have sex with them, as I understand it.
jokes.setup: Standard beautiful day description
jokes.punchline: Bart's bizarre misunderstanding of pollination
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of nature poetry with Bart's hilariously wrong understanding of biology. The phrase 'as I understand it' sells the confidence.

jokes.text: Too many wee ones!
jokes.setup: Orderly gym class selection process
jokes.punchline: Chaos as small children overwhelm the adult
jokes.explanation: Solid visual gag with good economy. The phrase 'wee ones' is funny in context of being overwhelmed.

jokes.text: Do you hear that, Bart? That was the tardy bell! 'Truant! Truant! Truant!' They'll all say!
jokes.setup: Being late to school
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's dramatic overreaction and specific fear of being called 'truant'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse neurosis with his specific fear and dramatic delivery of 'truant' repeated.

jokes.text: It's all right? It's all right. It's all right? It's all right.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to convince himself he's fine alone
jokes.punchline: The uncertain questioning tone revealing his insecurity
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's vulnerability beneath his bravado. The repetition with changing inflection works well.

jokes.text: However, since you have no collateral... I'm gonna have to break your legs in advance.
jokes.setup: Standard loan application process
jokes.punchline: Moe's preemptive violence as business practice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe character moment with dark humor and twisted business logic. 'In advance' makes it extra funny.

jokes.text: Gosh, Moe. I use these all the time. Couldn't you bash my head in?
jokes.setup: Homer objecting to having his legs broken
jokes.punchline: His counter-offer of head trauma as more convenient
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - treating violence like a menu option and prioritizing convenience over safety. Perfect character consistency.

jokes.text: Ballet? Dancing is for girls. Well, you should've gotten here earlier.
jokes.setup: Bart's masculine protest against ballet
jokes.punchline: The bureaucratic dismissal that offers no alternatives
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the episode's main plot with efficient dismissal of Bart's protests.

jokes.text: It says here that you grabbed a dog by the hind legs... and pushed him like a vacuum. In the third grade!
jokes.setup: Bad credit history discussion
jokes.punchline: Bizarre childhood incident being on permanent record
jokes.explanation: Incredibly specific and absurd image that's both disturbing and hilarious. The detail and the fact it's from third grade makes it perfect.

jokes.text: Good luck finding it. I'll take the numbers off. We'll find a house with no numbers. I'll take off my neighbor's numbers. So then we'll look for the house next to the house with no numbers.
jokes.setup: Bank threatening to take his house
jokes.punchline: The escalating logical spiral of house-hiding attempts
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation sequence where each solution creates a new problem. The logic is perfectly flawed and builds beautifully.

jokes.text: And to celebrate, I'm going to tilt my chair. Slanty.
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating solving his financial problems
jokes.punchline: His celebration is just tilting his chair and saying 'slanty'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - finding joy in the simplest things. 'Slanty' is inexplicably funny and perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Now, bring us extra chairs like a good 'blubber'-in-law. Time to fertilize the lawn. A couple of 500-pound bags should do it!
jokes.setup: Patty and Selma insulting Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's counter-insults about their weight
jokes.explanation: Standard insult comedy that escalates well but relies on weight jokes, which are fairly cheap.

jokes.text: Time to fertilize the lawn. A couple of 500-pound bags should do it!
jokes.setup: Homer muttering while getting chairs
jokes.punchline: Calling the sisters fertilizer and estimating their weight
jokes.explanation: Standard insult humor, gets the job done but not particularly creative

jokes.text: Be careful with my sisters' heads. Their necks are brittle.
jokes.setup: Homer handling the chairs roughly
jokes.punchline: Marge's matter-of-fact concern about fragile necks
jokes.explanation: Good understated Marge moment with unexpected medical detail

jokes.text: Montreal Morn.
jokes.setup: Homer making international coffee
jokes.punchline: The slightly exotic but not very impressive brand name
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of trying to seem sophisticated with mediocre products

jokes.text: Now we own you, like Siegfried owns Roy.
jokes.setup: The twins threatening Homer with blackmail
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific Vegas performer relationship metaphor
jokes.explanation: Excellent pop culture reference that's both specific and weirdly accurate to the power dynamic being described.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. All we have is Nescafé. I'm very, very sorry.
jokes.setup: Marge apologizing for coffee choice
jokes.punchline: Excessive apology for having Nescafé instead of fancy coffee
jokes.explanation: Mild character joke about Marge's over-apologizing

jokes.text: I'm giving them a foot massage. At least let me have that.
jokes.setup: Homer being forced to give foot rubs
jokes.punchline: His desperate attempt to maintain dignity by rebranding it
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer trying to preserve some dignity in humiliation. The desperation comes through well.

jokes.text: You can either be a fairy or a queen. It's wide open.
jokes.setup: Teacher explaining ballet roles
jokes.punchline: The unintentionally suggestive phrasing
jokes.explanation: Mild double entendre that works in context

jokes.text: Ballet is for the strong, the fierce, the determined. But for the sissies, never. Now, put on this fuchsia-tard. You are a fairy!
jokes.setup: Inspiring speech about ballet's strength and determination
jokes.punchline: Immediately undermined by the costume and fairy assignment
jokes.explanation: Good ironic contrast between the macho speech and the immediate contradiction. The teacher character works well.

jokes.text: Now, put on this fuchsia-tard. You are a fairy!
jokes.setup: Teacher just argued ballet isn't for sissies
jokes.punchline: Immediately hands Bart a bright pink outfit and calls him a fairy
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic contradiction immediately undermining the masculine ballet speech

jokes.text: But so many of your heroes wear tights. Batman, for example. And Magellan.
jokes.setup: Reasonable comparison to Batman wearing tights
jokes.punchline: The inexplicable addition of Magellan the explorer
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Simpsons' random historical references. Batman makes sense, Magellan is completely random and hilarious.

jokes.text: Toes twinkling. Look at me, girls. I'm doing ballet. And I love it!
jokes.setup: Bart getting caught up in ballet
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic transformation
jokes.explanation: Good character development moment with genuine enthusiasm

jokes.text: Am I wrong, or did it just get fatter in here?
jokes.setup: The twins enter a room
jokes.punchline: Immediate insult about Homer's presence affecting the environment
jokes.explanation: Good character line that shows their immediate hostility, though it's a fairly standard fat joke.

jokes.text: You can't spell 'obsequious' without I-O-U.
jokes.setup: Homer being subservient due to debt
jokes.punchline: Clever connection between the word 'obsequious' and debt terminology
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that's both intellectually clever and perfectly situational. Shows the twins can be witty beyond just mean.

jokes.text: Now say, 'I am Homer Simpson, the lowly dog.' In a dog voice!
jokes.setup: Homer already crawling on the floor
jokes.punchline: The additional humiliation of vocal impersonation
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe comedy that escalates the humiliation effectively, though fairly standard in concept.

jokes.text: He blew your savings on jack-o'-lanterns. You told!
jokes.setup: The revelation of Homer's investment
jokes.punchline: Homer's childish reaction to being tattled on
jokes.explanation: Good callback with Homer's childish reaction. 'Jack-o'-lanterns' makes it sound even sillier than pumpkins.

jokes.text: And at best, I was a B-plus dog.
jokes.setup: Homer's apology to Marge about failing as husband and provider
jokes.punchline: His academic grading of his performance as a dog
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - applying academic standards to degradation. The B-plus suggests he takes some pride in it.

jokes.text: Nobody make me any breakfast. A man so deeply in debt doesn't deserve it.
jokes.setup: Homer declaring he doesn't deserve breakfast
jokes.punchline: But then immediately ordering elaborate breakfast
jokes.explanation: Good setup for immediate contradiction showing Homer's lack of willpower

jokes.text: But I like to make you breakfast. In that case, I'll just have French toast with double butter and a side of bacon. But no powdered sugar, I don't deserve it. Maybe a little powdered sugar.
jokes.setup: Homer refusing breakfast due to shame
jokes.punchline: Immediately ordering an elaborate breakfast, then negotiating with himself about dessert
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - his complete inability to resist food despite his shame. The internal powdered sugar negotiation is brilliant.

jokes.text: No wonder I didn't hear about Bart being elected world's greatest sex machine.
jokes.setup: Homer realizing he's reading a fake newspaper
jokes.punchline: The absurdly inappropriate headline about his young son
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly inappropriate fake headline that's both shocking and perfectly absurd for a gag paper.

jokes.text: I didn't turn it on, I thought you did. No. Well, anyway, turn it off. It is off.
jokes.setup: Normal TV interaction
jokes.punchline: The TV is somehow on while being off - supernatural or broken
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment that hints at supernatural or surreal elements in the Simpsons universe.

jokes.text: So it's okay for me to enjoy this.
characters: Comic Book Guy or similar character
jokes.setup: Character noting the dancer is graceful yet masculine
jokes.punchline: The explicit permission he gives himself to enjoy ballet
jokes.explanation: Good commentary on masculinity and cultural permission to enjoy things. Character needs to rationalize his enjoyment.

jokes.text: This reminds me of the movie Fame, and to a lesser extent, the TV series, which was also called Fame.
jokes.setup: Man making cultural reference to Fame
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily specific distinction between movie and TV versions
jokes.explanation: Mild joke about overly pedantic pop culture references

jokes.text: Oh, wow, I can't believe my very first passenger is comedy legend Mel Brooks. I loved that movie Young Frankenstein. Scared the hell out of me.
jokes.setup: Homer meeting comedy legend Mel Brooks
jokes.punchline: Completely misunderstanding that Young Frankenstein is a comedy parody
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer cultural ignorance - meeting a comedy legend and completely missing the point of his most famous work.

jokes.text: Sure! Sure we had. Of course, we was caves. But every cave had a national anthem. I'll never forget my cave's national anthem. What was that national anthem? Let them all go to hell except Cave 76
jokes.setup: Discussion of ancient cave national anthems
jokes.punchline: The hilariously petty and specific cave nationalism
jokes.explanation: Great parody of the 2000-year-old-man routine with perfectly petty prehistoric nationalism. Cave 76 is specific enough to be funny.

jokes.text: Behold! The masked dancer is me, Bart! It is I who have won your acclaim!
jokes.setup: Bart succeeding anonymously at ballet
jokes.punchline: His dramatic, theatrical revelation and grandiose language
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's dramatic flair and need for recognition, though the reveal itself is predictable.

jokes.text: Go ahead and laugh. I took a chance and did what I wanted to do. And if that makes me a sissy, well, then, I guess I'm a sissy.
jokes.setup: Bart facing mockery for ballet
jokes.punchline: Dignified acceptance of consequences with gentle self-deprecation
jokes.explanation: Good character growth moment, more heartwarming than comedic but well-executed

jokes.text: Bart, use the ballet. Leap! Leap like you've never leapt before!
jokes.setup: Bart being chased by bullies
jokes.punchline: Teacher treating ballet as combat skill
jokes.explanation: Good action movie parody with ballet as martial arts

jokes.text: Well, as long as he's hurt.
jokes.setup: Bart taking a bad fall
jokes.punchline: Nelson's satisfied reaction to injury
jokes.explanation: Perfect Nelson line with maximum economy and character consistency

jokes.text: Why'd she just leave me here when I clearly need medical attention?
jokes.setup: Lisa leaving after her emotional speech
jokes.punchline: Bart's practical concern about his injuries
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Lisa's dramatic moment with practical concern

jokes.text: Tested, Homer. God tested Moses. And try to be nice to my sisters.
jokes.setup: Homer saying God teased Moses
jokes.punchline: Marge's gentle theological correction
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Marge - she gently corrects even Biblical misunderstandings while staying focused on family harmony.

jokes.text: Then I will hug some snakes. Yes! I will hug and kiss some poisonous snakes!
jokes.setup: Homer agreeing sarcastically to get along with the twins
jokes.punchline: The escalating metaphor of increasingly dangerous affection
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's sarcastic metaphor. The progression from hugging to kissing poisonous snakes builds well.

jokes.text: Now, that's sarcasm.
jokes.setup: Homer's sarcastic rant
jokes.punchline: His proud explanation of his own technique
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor with Homer's self-awareness of his rhetorical skills

jokes.text: Being a jerk. Minus a million!
jokes.setup: Driving test with petty point deductions
jokes.punchline: Homer's absurd counter-deduction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the point system with Homer fighting back

jokes.text: Oh, you're talking about she... Who's? What? She... What, this?
jokes.setup: Being caught smoking in government building
jokes.punchline: Their panicked, incoherent attempts to deny
jokes.explanation: Good panic reaction showing the twins caught off-guard

jokes.text: Yes. I am in flavor country. Both of them? It's a big country.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming ownership of cigarettes
jokes.punchline: The extension of the 'flavor country' cigarette marketing into actual geography
jokes.explanation: Brilliant extension of cigarette advertising language into literal geography. Homer treats marketing slogans as real places.

jokes.text: Ladies, I apologize. And you, sir, are worse than Hitler.
jokes.setup: Supervisor apologizing to Patty and Selma
jokes.punchline: The extreme escalation of calling a smoker worse than Hitler
jokes.explanation: Good absurd escalation though the Hitler comparison is somewhat cheap. The contrast between apologizing to the women and attacking Homer works.

jokes.text: don't make me picture your hides.
jokes.setup: Patty and Selma thanking Homer for saving their hides
jokes.punchline: Homer's disgusted literal interpretation
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment taking figurative language literally

jokes.text: On top of everything else... don't make me picture your hides.
jokes.setup: Patty and Selma saying he saved their hides
jokes.punchline: Homer's disgusted request not to visualize them literally
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's continued disgust even in their moment of gratitude. The literal interpretation of 'hides' works well.
